fields.name: Scotch Game
fields.description: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.d4 exd4 4.Nxd4 — A direct approach to seize the center. White immediately challenges Black's e5 pawn.

fields.name: Grand Prix Attack
fields.description: 1.e4 c5 2.Nc3 3.f4 — An aggressive setup against the Sicilian. White builds a kingside attack with f4-f5.

fields.name: Sicilian Najdorf
fields.description: 1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 d6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 Nf6 5.Nc3 a6 — The most popular Sicilian. 5...a6 prepares ...b5 and stops Nb5.

fields.name: King's Indian Defense
fields.description: 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 g6 3.Nc3 Bg7 4.e4 d6 5.Nf3 0-0 — Black gives up the center to fianchetto the Dragon Bishop and strike back with ...e5 or ...f5.

key_ideas: Capturing on d4 with ...exd4 gives White a central knight
4.Nxd4 regains the pawn with an active piece
content.explanation: The Scotch Game is one of the oldest openings. By playing 3.d4, White immediately challenges Black's central pawn and opens lines for development.
critical_moves: 3.d4 — The key move that defines the Scotch
common_mistakes: Delaying d4 allows Black to consolidate

content.instructions: Play the full Scotch Game sequence through move 4. Enter moves in order (both White and Black).

fields.title: After 4.Nxd4, Black plays 4...Bc5. What is White's best response?

content.question: After 4.Nxd4, Black plays 4...Bc5 attacking the knight. What is White's best response?

options.explanation: Develops and defends the knight, preparing Nb3
Avoids the bishop but blocks the b-pawn
Aggressive but allows ...Qh4+
Gives up the center for little compensation

key_ideas: f4 stakes a claim on e5 and supports f5
The kingside pawn storm is the main plan
content.explanation: The Grand Prix Attack aims for rapid development and a kingside attack. The f4-f5 thrust is the thematic break.
common_mistakes: Playing f4 too early before Nf3/Be2

fields.title: Build the ideal GPA setup (Nc3, f4, Nf3, Be2, 0-0)

content.instructions: Build the ideal Grand Prix Attack setup. Play the full sequence including Black's responses.

fields.title: Black plays ...d5. How do you respond?

content.question: In the Grand Prix, Black plays ...d5 to challenge the center. How do you respond?

options.explanation: Captures and opens the e-file
Blocking allows ...d4
Loses a piece to ...Nxd5
Premature; d5 must be addressed first

fields.title: Why Nc3 before f4? Flexible development

key_ideas: Nc3 keeps options open against ...e6, ...g6, and ...Nc6
f4 can follow once the knight controls d5
content.explanation: Playing 2.Nc3 before 3.f4 gives White flexibility. Against ...e6 (French-style), White can still play f4. Against ...g6, the GPA setup with f4 is ideal. The knight also eyes d5 and e4.
common_mistakes: Blocking the c-pawn with Nc3 then playing c3

fields.title: Black plays ...Bg4 pinning Nf3. How do you respond?

content.question: Black develops with ...Bg4, pinning your knight. What is White's best response?

options.explanation: Unpins and completes development; Bxf3 weakens Black's structure
Provokes ...Bxf3, but Be2 is more flexible
Leaves the knight pinned; risky
Castling into a pin is dangerous

fields.title: The f5 break — executing the attack

key_ideas: f5 is the thematic break once the GPA setup is complete
After f5 exf5, Bxf5 or exf5 with Ng5 both work
The attack targets f7 and the kingside
content.explanation: Once you have Nc3, f4, Nf3, Be2, and 0-0, the f5 break is the main plan. It opens lines and often leads to sacrifices on f7 or g7.

fields.title: Drill: Reach the f5 break position

content.instructions: Play the full sequence to the f5 break. Both sides complete development first.

fields.title: After f5, Black captures ...gxf5. Best recapture?

content.question: You play f5 and Black captures ...gxf5. What is White's best recapture?

options.explanation: Opens the e-file and clears the way for Ng5 or Bxf5
Good but exf5 is more aggressive
Loses time; the knight was well placed on f3
Too slow; recapture first

fields.title: The purpose of 5...a6 — stopping Nb5, preparing b5

content.explanation: The Najdorf is named after Miguel Najdorf. The move 5...a6 is multi-purpose: it prevents Nb5, prepares ...b5, and keeps options open.

fields.title: Play the first 5 moves of the Najdorf correctly

content.instructions: Play the full Najdorf sequence through move 5. You are Black.

fields.title: White plays 6.Bg5. What is Black's most combative response?

content.question: White plays 6.Bg5 pinning the knight. What is Black's most combative response?

options.explanation: Classical: prepares Be7, avoids doubled pawns, supports d5
Provokes Bxf6, conceding the bishop pair
Passive; knight belongs on c6 or d7 after ...e6
Possible but less common than e6

fields.title: Why give up the center? The Dragon Bishop explained

key_ideas: The Bg7 bishop attacks the long diagonal
content.explanation: The King's Indian gives White a big center. Black's plan is to let White overextend, then strike with ...e5 or ...f5. The g7 bishop is the 'Dragon Bishop' on the long diagonal.

fields.title: Build the full KID setup through move 5 (...0-0)

content.instructions: Build the King's Indian Defense setup. Play the full sequence through Black's castling.

fields.title: White plays d5 closing the center. What is Black's plan?

content.question: White plays d5 closing the center. What is Black's typical plan?

options.explanation: Retreats to support ...f5; the main plan in the KID
Opens the center when Black wants to attack on the flank
Solid but passive
Good idea but Ne8 typically comes first for ...f5
